CalifOrnia
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
March 26, 199O
Theresa TayLor
Attorney At Law
6940 Arabella Way
sacramento, cal-if ornia 95831
Re: ;::'.Ii:";:: I:;.llf:'nar Assistance
Dear Ms Tayl"or:
.You have requested informal assistance under the campaign
provisions of the Poriticar Reform Act.x/ 
Because the qulstions
you have asked are of a general nature, we treat your r6quest as
one for inforrnal assistance pursuant !o Regulation fezao[c1 (copy
enclosed) , rather than fornal advice.2/
QUESTIONS
1. Under what circumstances may a private firm or
professional association, which does not have a lobbyist \"rorking
in carifornia, provide an honorarium to a rnember ot tne califorii-a
Legislature, which might be used to pay a fine 1evied by a toca)-
or state governmental entity?
2. Under what circumstances nay a private firm, professional
association, nonprof i-t corporation, or inaiv:,duar rnake'a monetary
gift to a member of the Legislature, which rnight be used to pay a
fine levied by a l-ocaL or state entity?
3. Under $rhat circumstances may a private firrn or
professional association participate in the establ-ishment of an
L/ co.r"..,.".rt cod. Sections 81OOO-91-015. AII statutory
references are to the Government code unless otherwise indicateo.
commission regulations appear at 2 california code or neg;iations
Section 18000, et ses. A1I references to regulations ur6 to
Title 2, Division 6 of the california code oi Regulations.
2/ InformaL assj.stance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion oi formal writt6n advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 1s329(c) (3). )
'12S J Strcet, Suitc tlOt) . P.O. Box tlr)7 o S:rcranrcntt) Cr\ 958r)+-Ott('17 . (916) lll-iool)


Theresa D. Taylor
Page fvro
organization or committee designed to raise money for the express
purpose of making gifts to a legislator for the purpose of heJ-ping
to pay a fine ]evied by a local or state government entity?
4. May a member of the Legislature use funds received as
1egal honoraria or gifts to pay fines levied by a state or local
governmental entity?
CONCLUSI ONS
Before addressing your specific questions, we would like to
clarify that the nature of a payment received bY an elected
officei is determined by the purpose for which the payment is
made. Eor example, a payrnent which meets the definition of
ttcontr ibutionrr because it is made for "political 
purposesrr may not
be characterized as a ngiftrt or an rrhonorarj-umrr in order to avoid
the Act's lirnitations on contributions. 
The Act contains
definitions of the terms rr contributionrr and 'rgift. rr The term
"honorariun" is defined by Cornrnission Regulation l-8540 (copy
enclosedl .3/
It appears from the questions you have asked that the payment
in question is intended to be for the purpose of assj'sting a
member of the Legislature pay a fine imposed for violations of the
California Efections Code. The violations were in connection with
the nenber's duties as an officeholder. 
Assuming this is the
case, the payment r"/ould be a rrcontribution. rr It nay not be
characterized as an trhonorariumrt or a rtgif t. It
Following are answers to your specif i.c questionsl
1. There is no lirnit on the amount of gifts and honoraria a
firm ruay pay an elected officer, as long as the gifts and
honoraria are not in connection with a speech. article, 
or
published work on a sub'i ect relatinq to the qovernmental process.
Hov/ever, as mentioned above, if the purpose of the paynent is to
assist the elected officer in paying a fine imposed for a
violation of the Elections Code, the paynent is a rrcontribution,'r
and may not be characterized as an rrhonorarium. r'
Regulation 1854o was adopted by the Cornmission in June, 1989.
It has been submitted to the Office of Administrative Law and l^ras
disapproved for technical problerns. The regulation wiII be
resubrnitted for review by the Office of Adninistrative Law in the
near future. 
In the rneant j.rne, this regulation embodies the
Commission,s policy deternination concerning interpretation of
Section 85400.


Theresa D. Taylor
Page Three
2. A payment to an elected officer for the purpose of paying
a fine levied by a }ocaL or state entity most l-ikely lrould meet
the definition of t'contribution,rt not t'gift. " If the payment is
intended to assist the elected officer in paying a fine as
described above, the payment would be a 'rcontribution . I' It nay
not be characterized as a "gift." 
A f irrn rnay make contributions
of no more than $1,000 to a candidate during a fiscal year (JuIy I
through June 30).
3. A person who receives noney from others for the purpose
of assisting an elected off j,cer i-n paying a fine as described
above would be receiving rr contributions. rr A rrrecipient cornmitteerl
would be created when the person receives $1,0oo or more for this
purpose during a calendar year. For purposes of the Act's
contribution tinitations, 
the conmittee would be a "political
committee. t' A political 
committee may make contributions of no
more than $2,5o0 to a candidate during a f iscal- year. If the
commj-ttee is Isponsoredrr by an organization, contributions rnade by
the corunittee must be aggregated with contributions rnade by the
sponsoring organization. An organization and its sponsored
political 
committee rnay make contributions of no more than $2,500
to a candidate during a fiscal year.
4. An honorariun or a nonetary gift received by an elected
officer is "incone" to the e]ected officer. 
There are no
restrictions on the use of an elected officer's personal funds.
Ho$rever, as discussed above, a pa)rment which actually is made for
political 
purposes and is therefore a contribution (such as a
payment to pay a fine imposed under the Elections code), may not
be characterized as an honorariurn or a gift.
ANALYSIS
With regard to conclusions 1, 2 and 4, a payment received by
an elected officer is a "contributionrt unless it is clear frorn
the surrounding circu:nstances that it is not made for pol j.tical,
purposes. (Section 82015. )
A payment is made for political 
purposes if it is:
Received by or rnade at the behest of a candidate, unl,ess
it is clear fron surrounding circumstances that the palment
was received or made at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as
an office hoIder....
(Regulation I82l-s (a) (2) (A).)


Theresa D. TayLor
Page Four
It appears from the nature of your questions, and because of
the similarity of your questions to another request for assistance
we have received (Brand Advice Letter, No. I-90-I-06, copy
encJ-osed), that the payment about which you are inquiring j.s
intended for the purpose of assisting an elected officer to pay a
fine imposed for vj,olations of the california Elections code. The
violations clearly vere in connection with the efected officer,s
rrstatus as an office holder.rr Therefore, the payments are for a
ripolitical purpose,rr and are rrcontr ibutions . rr Contributions to
candidates from a rrpersonrr (such as a firm) are linited to no nore
than $1,000 during a fisca] year.
our concl,usion does not change if the payment is an
rrhonorarium.rr An honorariurn is a payment for a speech, article or
published $rork. (Regulation 18540(b).) Section 85400 limits the
honoraria an elected officer nay receive for any speech, article
or published work on the governmental process to $I,0OO per single
source in a calendar year.
An honorarium is presumed to be a gift, 
(Regulation
18540(c).) An honorarium v/ould be considered income, and not a
giftr only if the official- provides full, and adequate
consideration in return for the honorarium payment. (Regulation
18540(c).) If an official 
clairns that an honorarium payment is
income rather than a gift, the official 
has the burden of showing
that she provided fuII and adequate consideration in exchange for
the honorarium. (Regul,ation 1854o (c) . )
If an elected officer in fact provides full and adequate
consideration i.n exchange for an honorariun payment, the pa)rment
wil-} be rrinconerr even if the officer ultimately uses the money to
pay a fine for violation of the Elections code. In this case, the
paynent woul-d not be a |tcontribution. rl
In the situation you have described, the rrhonorariumtr paynent
would appear to be a contribution to the elected officer if 
(1)
the elected officer does not provide fuII and adequate
consideration in return, and (2) the purpose of the pa)rment is to
assist the official 
in paying a fine for viol,ation of the
Elections code. Thus, j-t is not possible for a donor to avoid the
contribution linits of the Act sirnply by paying an rrhonorariunfi to
the elected off icer.
Regarding conclusion 3, Section 82013(a) provides:
trComnitteert neans any person or cornbination of persons \^Iho
directty or indirectly. . .:
(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand doll-ars
($I,000) or nore in a calendar year....


Theresa D. Taylor
Page Five
Because ttre money you anticipate raising from other people is for
the purpose of rnaking contributions to an e1ected officer, the
money you receive would be rrcontributions. " 
When you receive
contributions totaling $1,,000 or more during a calendar year, you
must register and file disclosure reports as a recipient committee
pursuant to Chapter 4 of the Act.
For purposes of the Act's contribution 1i-mitations, a
recipient comrnittee is either a 'rpolitical committeerr or a rtbroad
based political 
committee. "
A ttpolitical comrnittee" is:
A committee of persons who receives contributions from
t$/o or more persons and acting in concert makes
contributions to candidates.
(Section 85102 (c) . )
A rrbroad based political 
cornmitteerr is:
A committee of persons which has been in existence for
more than six months, receives contributions fron one
hundred or more persons, and acting in concert makes
contributions to five or more candidates.
(Section 85I02 (d) . )
The Act provides that a "political 
conmittee'r may contribute
no more than $2,500 to a candidate during any fiscal year, and
that a rrbroad based political 
cornmittee'r may contribute no more
than $5,000 to a candidate during any fiscal year.
In addition, a recipient committee would be a Isponsored
committeerr if any of the following criteria are met:
(a) trsponsored cornmittee[ neans a committee, other
than a candidate controlled comrnittee, which has one
or more sponsors. Any person, except a candidate or
other individual, may sponsor a committee.
(b) A person sponsors a comnittee if any of the
following apply:
(I) 
The conrnittee receives al,L or nearly aII of
its contributions fron the person or its nembers,
officers, employees, or shareholders.


Theresa D. Taylor
Page six
(2) The person collects contributions for the
committee by use of payroll deductj-on or dues from
its mernbers, officers, or ernployees.
(3) The person alone or in conbination lrith other
organizations provides all or nearly aII of the
adninistrative services for the cornmittee.
(4) The person, alone or in combination with other
organizations, sets the policies for soliciting
contributions or rnaking expenditures of committee
funds .
(Section 82048.7 .)
Enclosed for your inforrnation are:
-- The Political 
Reform Act of 1974 As Amended To
January I, 1990.
-- Regulation 1821,5 and proposed Regulation 18540.
-- The FPPC trlnformation l'lanual on campaign Disclosure
Provisions Of The Politica1 Reform Act.rl
If you have additional questions, please call, me at (916)
322-5662.
Sincerely,
Division Ch j.ef ,
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division
